rows.answer:
# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (any kind — glass, paper, shuttered openings):**

| Region | Population | % with windows | Window-havers |
|---|---|---|---|
| China (paper/lattice windows common) | 330M | ~45% | 149M |
| India (hot climate, many open dwellings) | 170M | ~25% | 43M |
| Europe (glass windows widespread by 1800) | 200M | ~65% | 130M |
| Africa (traditional dwellings, few windows) | 100M | ~10% | 10M |
| Americas | 30M | ~35% | 11M |
| Rest of Asia/Middle East | 170M | ~30% | 51M |
| **Total** | **1,000M** | **~39%** | **~390M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time** (for those with windows):

- Agricultural workers (~200M): ~5 min/day (mostly outdoors)
- Urban workers/craftspeople (~70M): ~12 min/day (work near windows for light)
- Wealthy/leisured class (~10M): ~20 min/day
- Children with windows (~70M): ~5 min/day
- Elderly (~40M): ~15 min/day

**Weighted average ≈ 7.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$390{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 7.5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} \div 1{,}440 \text{ min/day}$$

$$= 390{,}000{,}000 \times 1.901 \approx 741{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## Key reasoning notes
- In 1800, natural light from windows was the *primary* indoor illumination, so people positioned themselves near windows for tasks (sewing, reading, crafting), leading to frequent outward glances.
- Most people were agricultural, spending the bulk of daylight hours outdoors — limiting window time.
- I include paper, oiled-cloth, and open-shuttered windows, not just glass.

## My estimate: **700,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | Window access | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~80% | 160M |
| China | ~330M | ~60% | 198M |
| India/S. Asia | ~200M | ~40% | 80M |
| Japan | ~30M | ~70% | 21M |
| Middle East | ~30M | ~60% | 18M |
| SE Asia | ~40M | ~40% | 16M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~25% | 25M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~40% | 12M |
| **Total** | **~1B** | **~53%** | **~530M** |

I'm counting any framed wall opening (not just glass) that one can look through.

**Average daily window-gazing time (~3.5 min/day):**
- Most people were agricultural laborers spending the bulk of daylight outdoors
- But poor artificial lighting meant indoor tasks (sewing, cooking) clustered near windows
- Checking weather, watching for arrivals, idle glances add up
- Children, elderly, and the infirm gaze more; field laborers almost never do
- ~7 hours/day spent indoors while awake → looking through a window ~0.8% of that time ≈ 3.5 minutes

## Calculation

$$530{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 3.5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} \div 1440 \text{ min/day}$$

$$= \frac{530{,}000{,}000 \times 1{,}277.5}{1{,}440}$$

## My estimate: **470,000,000 days**

rows.answer:
# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**People with access to glass (or functionally transparent) windows:**

| Region | Population | % with glass windows | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Western Europe | ~150M | ~70% | ~105M |
| Eastern Europe/Russia | ~50M | ~30% | ~15M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~15% | ~4.5M |
| China/Japan | ~340M | ~1% | ~3M |
| India | ~200M | ~1% | ~2M |
| Rest of world | ~180M | ~1.5% | ~3M |
| **Total** | | | **~133M** |

Note: I'm counting transparent windows (glass), not open holes or opaque shutters, since "looking *through*" a window implies a transparent barrier.

**Average daily window-gazing time (~3.5 minutes/day):**
- Agricultural laborers (outdoors most of the day): ~1 min/day
- Artisans/craftspeople (indoor work): ~3 min/day
- Housewives/domestic: ~5 min/day
- Wealthy, elderly, children, sick: ~8–15 min/day
- No electric light → limited evening gazing; no TV/internet → window was *one of the few* passive diversions, but long working hours dominated life

## Calculation

Per person per year: 3.5 min/day × 365 days = 1,278 min/year ≈ **0.888 days/year**

Total: 133,000,000 × 0.888 ≈ **118,000,000 days**

## My estimate: **115,000,000 days**

This is well below 530,000,000 — even aggressive assumptions (broader window definitions, longer gazing times) keep the figure under ~300 million.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**Excluding infants (under ~1 year):** ~920 million people capable of meaningfully gazing through a window.

**Fraction with window access (~58%):** ≈ 534 million people
- **Europe (~200M):** ~80% had glass windows → 160M
- **East Asia (~350M):** ~55% had usable openings (openable paper screens, unglazed windows) → 193M
- **South Asia (~200M):** ~40% had wall openings functioning as windows → 80M
- **Middle East/N. Africa (~50M):** ~50% → 25M
- **Sub-Saharan Africa (~70M):** ~20% → 14M
- **Americas (~30M):** ~40% → 12M

**Average daily window-gazing time: ~4 minutes**
- Most people were agricultural laborers, spending the majority of daylight outdoors.
- Indoor daylight time averaged ~6–7 hours; roughly **1%** of that was spent with gaze directed *through* a window (checking weather, watching for visitors, glancing up from work done near a window for light).
- Women, craftspeople, elderly, and winter months push this up; outdoor laborers and small/scarce windows push it down.

## Calculation

| Step | Value |
|------|-------|
| People with window access | 534,000,000 |
| Minutes/person/day gazing | 4 |
| Person-minutes per day | 2,136,000,000 |
| × 365 days | 779,640,000,000 person-minutes/year |
| ÷ 1,440 min/day | **541,417,000 person-days** |

## My estimate: **540,000,000 person-days**

This exceeds the 530,000,000 threshold — though only modestly, reflecting the genuine knife-edge uncertainty in parameters like average gazing duration and window access rates.

I should also clarify what "looking through a window" means—whether it's just gazing out or includes any visual attention through glass while doing other things. I'll assume it's any deliberate looking, including quick glances. Sticking with 400 million person-days as my estimate.

Let me verify this makes sense: that's roughly 1.1 million person-years out of a billion people, or about 0.1% of total human time. # Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (broadly defined, including paper/lattice/shuttered openings):**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 80% | 160M |
| China | 300M | 60% | 180M |
| India/S. Asia | 200M | 40% | 80M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 100M | 20% | 20M |
| Americas, Japan, SE Asia, MENA, etc. | 200M | ~50% | 96M |
| **Total** | **1B** | | **~536M** |

Removing infants/toddlers (~12%) who don't meaningfully gaze through windows: **~470 million people**

**Average daily window-gazing time (~3.5 min/day):**
- ~70% rural farmers: ~2 min/day (quick weather/visitor checks)
- ~15% urban workers: ~5 min/day (workshop/shop glances)
- ~5% wealthier/leisure class: ~10 min/day
- ~5% elderly/homebound: ~15 min/day
- ~5% very young children: ~0 min/day
- **Weighted average ≈ 3.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$470{,}000{,}000 \times 3.5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1{,}440 \;\text{min/day} \approx 417{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

**Sanity check:** This equals ~1.6 minutes/day averaged over *all* humans — a modest, believable fraction of daily life for a predominantly outdoor-laboring population.

## My estimate: **400,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1,000,000,000

**Fraction with access to windows:**
| Region | Population | % with windows | Effective |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Europe | ~200M | ~75% | 150M |
| China | ~330M | ~65% (paper windows common) | 215M |
| India | ~200M | ~45% | 90M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~20% | 20M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~30% | 9M |
| Rest | ~140M | ~45% | 63M |
| **Total** | | | **~547M** |

Excluding children under ~3 (high birth rates → ~10%): **~490 million effective gazers**

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- Farmers/outdoor workers (~60%): ~2 min/day (outdoors most of daylight hours)
- Indoor domestic/craft workers (~25%): ~5 min/day
- Wealthy/elderly/confined (~5%): ~20 min/day
- Children 3–14 (~10%): ~4 min/day

**Weighted average: ~4.3 minutes/day**

Key considerations: no artificial lighting (no nighttime gazing), no competing screens, but windows were small and people worked relentlessly. Weather-checking and glancing toward light sources add up.

## Calculation

$$490{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{4.3}{1440} \;\text{(day-fraction)} \approx 534{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **550,000,000 days**

(Rounded slightly upward from the arithmetic to account for the likelihood that casual, unconscious glances through windows — checking weather, looking toward visitors, drawn by light — are easy to undercount.)

rows.answer:
# Estimating Total Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**Regional breakdown of window access and gazing time:**

| Region | Population | % with windows | Avg. min/day gazing |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe | ~200M | 70% | 6 |
| China/Japan/Korea | ~350M | 50% | 5 |
| South/SE Asia & India | ~250M | 30% | 4 |
| Middle East/N. Africa | ~40M | 40% | 5 |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | ~70M | 15% | 3 |
| Americas & other | ~40M | 30% | 5 |

**Key reasoning:**
- ~70–80% of people were agricultural laborers, outdoors most of the day
- Even rural homes in Europe and East Asia (paper windows) had some window openings
- Tropical/sub-Saharan dwellings had fewer formal windows
- Infants, the blind, and those perpetually outdoors contribute ~0
- Women doing domestic work and the elderly/children indoors drove most window-gazing

## Calculation

Summing regional contributions gives **~2,200 million person-minutes per day**.

Over a full year:
$$2{,}200{,}000{,}000 \times 365 \div 1{,}440 \approx 557{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

Cross-checking: this is equivalent to ~2.5 minutes/day averaged over the *entire* world population — which feels right given that most people had limited indoor time and small/no windows.

## My Estimate

**600,000,000 person-days**
